name: python-bitcoinlib

metadata.name: python-bitcoinlib
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: GNU Lesser General Public License v3 or later (LGPLv3+)
metadata.summary: The Swiss Army Knife of the Bitcoin protocol.

documentation:
# python-bitcoinlib

This Python3 library provides an easy interface to the bitcoin data
structures and protocol. The approach is low-level and "ground up", with a
focus on providing tools to manipulate the internals of how Bitcoin works.

"The Swiss Army Knife of the Bitcoin protocol." - Wladimir J. van der Laan


## Requirements

    sudo apt-get install libssl-dev

The RPC interface, `bitcoin.rpc`, should work with Bitcoin Core v24.0 or later.
Older versions may work but there do exist some incompatibilities.


## Structure

Everything consensus critical is found in the modules under bitcoin.core. This
rule is followed pretty strictly, for instance chain parameters are split into
consensus critical and non-consensus-critical.

    bitcoin.core            - Basic core definitions, datastructures, and
                              (context-independent) validation
    bitcoin.core.key        - ECC pubkeys
    bitcoin.core.script     - Scripts and opcodes
    bitcoin.core.scripteval - Script evaluation/verification
    bitcoin.core.serialize  - Serialization

In the future the bitcoin.core may use the Satoshi sourcecode directly as a
library. Non-consensus critical modules include the following:

    bitcoin          - Chain selection
    bitcoin.base58   - Base58 encoding
    bitcoin.bloom    - Bloom filters (incomplete)
    bitcoin.net      - Network communication (in flux)
    bitcoin.messages - Network messages (in flux)
    bitcoin.rpc      - Bitcoin Core RPC interface support
    bitcoin.wallet   - Wallet-related code, currently Bitcoin address and
                       private key support

Effort has been made to follow the Satoshi source relatively closely, for
instance Python code and classes that duplicate the functionality of
corresponding Satoshi C++ code uses the same naming conventions: CTransaction,
CBlockHeader, nValue etc. Otherwise Python naming conventions are followed.


## Mutable vs. Immutable objects

Like the Bitcoin Core codebase CTransaction is immutable and
CMutableTransaction is mutable; unlike the Bitcoin Core codebase this
distinction also applies to COutPoint, CTxIn, CTxOut, and CBlock.


## Endianness Gotchas

Rather confusingly Bitcoin Core shows transaction and block hashes as
little-endian hex rather than the big-endian the rest of the world uses for
SHA256. python-bitcoinlib provides the convenience functions x() and lx() in
bitcoin.core to convert from big-endian and little-endian hex to raw bytes to
accommodate this. In addition see b2x() and b2lx() for conversion from bytes to
big/little-endian hex.


## Module import style

While not always good style, it's often convenient for quick scripts if
`import *` can be used. To support that all the modules have `__all__` defined
appropriately.


# Example Code

See `examples/` directory. For instance this example creates a transaction
spending a pay-to-script-hash transaction output:

    $ PYTHONPATH=. examples/spend-p2sh-txout.py
    <hex-encoded transaction>


## Selecting the chain to use

Do the following:

    import bitcoin
    bitcoin.SelectParams(NAME)

Where NAME is one of 'testnet', 'mainnet', 'signet', or 'regtest'. The chain currently
selected is a global variable that changes behavior everywhere, just like in
the Satoshi codebase.


## Unit tests

Under bitcoin/tests using test data from Bitcoin Core. To run them:

    python3 -m unittest discover

Alternately, if Tox (see https://tox.readthedocs.org/) is available on your
system, you can run unit tests for multiple Python versions:

    ./runtests.sh

HTML coverage reports can then be found in the htmlcov/ subdirectory.


## Documentation

Sphinx documentation is in the "doc" subdirectory. Run "make help" from there
to see how to build. You will need the Python "sphinx" package installed.

Currently this is just API documentation generated from the code and
docstrings. Higher level written docs would be useful, perhaps starting with
much of this README. Pages are written in reStructuredText and linked from
index.rst.

methods.name: bitcoin.bech32.CBech32Data.__init__
documentation:
Initialize from bech32-encoded string

Note: subclasses put your initialization routines here, but ignore the
argument - that's handled by __new__(), and .from_bytes() will call
__init__() with None in place of the string.

documentation:
Convert to bytes instance

Note that it's the data represented that is converted; the checkum and
witver is not included.

documentation: Convert to string

Bech32-encoded data

Includes a witver and checksum.

Convert a redeemScript to a P2SH address

Convenience function: equivalent to P2SHBitcoinAddress.from_scriptPubKey(redeemScript.to_p2sh_scriptPubKey())

Convert a scriptPubKey to a P2SH address

Raises CBitcoinAddressError if the scriptPubKey isn't of the correct
form.

methods.name: bitcoin.wallet.P2PKHBitcoinAddress.from_pubkey
documentation:
Create a P2PKH bitcoin address from a pubkey

Raises CBitcoinAddressError if pubkey is invalid, unless accept_invalid
is True.

The pubkey must be a bytes instance; CECKey instances are not accepted.

methods.name: bitcoin.wallet.P2PKHBitcoinAddress.from_scriptPubKey
documentation:
Convert a scriptPubKey to a P2PKH address

Raises CBitcoinAddressError if the scriptPubKey isn't of the correct
form.

accept_non_canonical_pushdata - Allow non-canonical pushes (default True)

accept_bare_checksig          - Treat bare-checksig as P2PKH scriptPubKeys (default True)

modules.name: bitcoin.tests.fakebitcoinproxy
documentation:
`FakeBitcoinProxy` allows for unit testing of code that normally uses bitcoin
RPC without requiring a running bitcoin node.

`FakeBitcoinProxy` has an interface similar to `bitcoin.rpc.Proxy`, but does not
connect to a local bitcoin RPC node. Hence, `FakeBitcoinProxy` is similar to a
mock for the RPC tests.

`FakeBitcoinProxy` does _not_ implement a full bitcoin RPC node. Instead, it
currently implements only a subset of the available RPC commands. Test setup is
responsible for populating a `FakeBitcoinProxy` object with reasonable mock
data.

:author: Bryan Bishop <kanzure@gmail.com>

functions.name: bitcoin.tests.fakebitcoinproxy.make_address_from_passphrase
documentation:
Create a Bitcoin address from a passphrase. The passphrase is hashed and
then used as the secret bytes to construct the CBitcoinSecret.

functions.name: bitcoin.tests.fakebitcoinproxy.make_txout
documentation:
Make a CTxOut object based on the parameters. Otherwise randomly generate a
CTxOut to represent a transaction output.

```
param amount

amount in satoshis
```

functions.name: bitcoin.tests.fakebitcoinproxy.make_blocks_from_blockhashes
documentation:
Create some block data suitable for FakeBitcoinProxy to consume during
instantiation.

documentation:
Construct an entry for the list of commands that will be passed as a batch


documentation:
Construct a new address based on a passphrase template. As more

documentation: Completely unrealistic fake version of importaddress.

methods.name: bitcoin.tests.fakebitcoinproxy.FakeBitcoinProxy.fundrawtransaction
documentation: Make up some inputs for the given transaction.
This was implemented a long time ago and it's possible that this does not
match the current behavior of fundrawtransaction.

methods.name: bitcoin.tests.fakebitcoinproxy.FakeBitcoinProxy.signrawtransaction
documentation:
This method does not actually sign the transaction, but it does return
a transaction based on the given transaction.

methods.name: bitcoin.tests.fakebitcoinproxy.FakeBitcoinProxy.sendrawtransaction
documentation:
Pretend to broadcast and relay the transaction. Return the txid of the
given transaction.

methods.name: bitcoin.tests.fakebitcoinproxy.FakeBitcoinProxy._batch
documentation:
Process a bunch of requests all at once. This mimics the _batch RPC
feature found in python-bitcoinlib and bitcoind RPC.

This is an alternative to using `bitcoin.rpc.Proxy` in tests. This class
can store a number of blocks and transactions, which can then be retrieved
by calling various "RPC" methods.

functions.name: bitcoin.bloom.MurmurHash3
documentation:
MurmurHash3 (x86_32)

Used for bloom filters. See http://code.google.com/p/smhasher/source/browse/trunk/MurmurHash3.cpp

methods.name: bitcoin.bloom.CBloomFilter.__init__
documentation:
Create a new bloom filter

The filter will have a given false-positive rate when filled with the
given number of elements.

Note that if the given parameters will result in a filter outside the
bounds of the protocol limits, the filter created will be as close to
the given parameters as possible within the protocol limits. This will
apply if nFPRate is very low or nElements is unreasonably high.

nTweak is a constant which is added to the seed value passed to the
hash function It should generally always be a random value (and is
largely only exposed for unit testing)

nFlags should be one of the UPDATE_* enums (but not _MASK)

methods.name: bitcoin.bloom.CBloomFilter.insert
documentation:
Insert an element in the filter.

elem may be a COutPoint or bytes

methods.name: bitcoin.bloom.CBloomFilter.contains
documentation:
Test if the filter contains an element

elem may be a COutPoint or bytes

modules.name: bitcoin.rpc
documentation:
Bitcoin Core RPC support

By default this uses the standard library `json` module. By monkey patching,
a different implementation can be used instead, at your own risk:

```pycon
>>> import simplejson
>>> import bitcoin.rpc
>>> bitcoin.rpc.json = simplejson
```

(`simplejson` is the externally maintained version of the same module and
thus better optimized but perhaps less stable.)
Copyright (C) 2007 Jan-Klaas Kollhof
Copyright (C) 2011-2018 The python-bitcoinlib developers
This file is part of python-bitcoinlib.
It is subject to the license terms in the LICENSE file found in the top-level
directory of this distribution.
No part of python-bitcoinlib, including this file, may be copied, modified,
propagated, or distributed except according to the terms contained in the
LICENSE file.

functions.name: bitcoin.rpc.unhexlify_str
documentation:
Converts a string containing hexadecimal encoding into a bytes-object.

It works by encoding the given string h as ASCII, then interpreting each of
its two-character ASCII tuples as bytes:
    - "00" to byte 0
    - "ff" to byte 255
    - "FF" also to byte 255

The string must only contain characters in the ranges 0-9, a-f and A-F.

If the string contains characters not in ASCII,
    UnicodeEncodeError is raised.
If the string contains out-of-range ASCII characters,
    binascii.Error is raised.
If number of encoded ASCII bytes is odd,
    binascii.Error is raised.

functions.name: bitcoin.rpc.hexlify_str
documentation:
Given an arbitrary bytes-like object, returns a string (that would encode
as ASCII) containing the hex-representation of the bytes-like object.

Always succeeds.

classes.name: bitcoin.rpc.JSONRPCError
documentation:
JSON-RPC protocol error base class

Subclasses of this class also exist for specific types of errors; the set
of all subclasses is by no means complete.

classes.name: bitcoin.rpc.BaseProxy
documentation:
Base JSON-RPC proxy class. Contains only private methods; do not use
directly.

classes.name: bitcoin.rpc.RawProxy
documentation:
Low-level proxy to a bitcoin JSON-RPC service

Unlike `Proxy`, no conversion is done besides parsing JSON. As far as
Python is concerned, you can call any method; `JSONRPCError` will be
raised if the server does not recognize it.

classes.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.__init__
documentation:
Create a proxy object

If `service_url` is not specified, the username and password are read
out of the file `btc_conf_file`. If `btc_conf_file` is not
specified, `~/.bitcoin/bitcoin.conf` or equivalent is used by
default.  The default port is set according to the chain parameters in
use: mainnet, testnet, signet, or regtest.

Usually no arguments to `Proxy()` are needed; the local bitcoind will
be used.

`timeout` - timeout in seconds before the HTTP interface times out

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.call
documentation: Call an RPC method by name and raw (JSON encodable) arguments

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.dumpprivkey
documentation: Return the private key matching an address

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.fundrawtransaction
documentation:
Add inputs to a transaction until it has enough in value to meet its out value.

include_watching - Also select inputs which are watch only

Returns dict:

{'tx':        Resulting tx,
 'fee':       Fee the resulting transaction pays,
 'changepos': Position of added change output, or -1,
}

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.generate
documentation:
DEPRECATED (will be removed in bitcoin-core v0.19)

Mine blocks immediately (before the RPC call returns)

numblocks - How many blocks are generated immediately.

Returns iterable of block hashes generated.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.generatetoaddress
documentation:
Mine blocks immediately (before the RPC call returns) and
allocate block reward to passed address. Replaces deprecated
"generate(self,numblocks)" method.

numblocks - How many blocks are generated immediately.
addr     - Address to receive block reward (CBitcoinAddress instance)

Returns iterable of block hashes generated.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getaccountaddress
documentation:
Return the current Bitcoin address for receiving payments to this
account.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getbalance
documentation:
Get the balance

account - The selected account. Defaults to "*" for entire wallet. It
may be the default account using "".

minconf - Only include transactions confirmed at least this many times.
(default=1)

include_watchonly - Also include balance in watch-only addresses (see 'importaddress')
(default=False)

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getbestblockhash
documentation: Return hash of best (tip) block in longest block chain.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getblockheader
documentation:
Get block header <block_hash>

verbose - If true a dict is returned with the values returned by
          getblockheader that are not in the block header itself
          (height, nextblockhash, etc.)

Raises IndexError if block_hash is not valid.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getblock
documentation:
Get block <block_hash>

Raises IndexError if block_hash is not valid.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getblockcount
documentation: Return the number of blocks in the longest block chain

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getblockhash
documentation:
Return hash of block in best-block-chain at height.

Raises IndexError if height is not valid.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getinfo
documentation: Return a JSON object containing various state info

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getmininginfo
documentation: Return a JSON object containing mining-related information

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getnewaddress
documentation:
Return a new Bitcoin address for receiving payments.

If account is not None, it is added to the address book so payments
received with the address will be credited to account.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getrawchangeaddress
documentation:
Returns a new Bitcoin address, for receiving change.

This is for use with raw transactions, NOT normal use.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getrawmempool
documentation: Return the mempool

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getrawtransaction
documentation:
Return transaction with hash txid

Raises IndexError if transaction not found.

verbose - If true a dict is returned instead with additional
information on the transaction.

block_hash - Hash of the block containing the transaction
(required when transaction not currently indexed by Bitcoin Core)

Note that if all txouts are spent and the transaction index is not
enabled the transaction may not be available.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.getreceivedbyaddress
documentation:
Return total amount received by given a (wallet) address

Get the amount received by <address> in transactions with at least
[minconf] confirmations.

Works only for addresses in the local wallet; other addresses will
always show zero.

addr    - The address. (CBitcoinAddress instance)

minconf - Only include transactions confirmed at least this many times.
(default=1)

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.gettransaction
documentation:
Get detailed information about in-wallet transaction txid

Raises IndexError if transaction not found in the wallet.

FIXME: Returned data types are not yet converted.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.gettxout
documentation:
Return details about an unspent transaction output.

Raises IndexError if outpoint is not found or was spent.

includemempool - Include mempool txouts

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.importaddress
documentation: Adds an address or pubkey to wallet without the associated privkey.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.listunspent
documentation:
Return unspent transaction outputs in wallet

Outputs will have between minconf and maxconf (inclusive)
confirmations, optionally filtered to only include txouts paid to
addresses in addrs.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.lockunspent
documentation: Lock or unlock outpoints

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.sendrawtransaction
documentation:
Submit transaction to local node and network.

allowhighfees - Allow even if fees are unreasonably high.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.sendmany
documentation:
Send amount to given addresses.

payments - dict with {address: amount}

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.sendtoaddress
documentation: Send amount to a given address

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.signrawtransaction
documentation:
Sign inputs for transaction

FIXME: implement options

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.signrawtransactionwithwallet
documentation:
Sign inputs for transaction
    bicoincore >= 0.17.x

FIXME: implement options

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.submitblock
documentation:
Submit a new block to the network.

params is optional and is currently ignored by bitcoind. See
https://en.bitcoin.it/wiki/BIP_0022 for full specification.

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.validateaddress
documentation: Return information about an address

methods.name: bitcoin.rpc.Proxy.unlockwallet
documentation:
Stores the wallet decryption key in memory for 'timeout' seconds.

password - The wallet passphrase.

timeout - The time to keep the decryption key in seconds.
(default=60)

Proxy to a bitcoin RPC service

Unlike `RawProxy`, data is passed as `bitcoin.core` objects or packed
bytes, rather than JSON or hex strings. Not all methods are implemented
yet; you can use `call` to access missing ones in a forward-compatible
way. Assumes Bitcoin Core version >= v0.16.0; older versions mostly work,
but there are a few incompatibilities.

Select the core chain parameters to use

Don't use this directly, use bitcoin.SelectParams() instead so both

functions.name: bitcoin.core.CheckTransaction

Basic transaction checks that don't depend on any context.

Raises CheckTransactionError

documentation:
Check a proof-of-work

Raises CheckProofOfWorkError

functions.name: bitcoin.core.CheckBlockHeader
documentation:
Context independent CBlockHeader checks.

fCheckPoW - Check proof-of-work.

cur_time  - Current time. Defaults to time.time()

Raises CBlockHeaderError if block header is invalid.

functions.name: bitcoin.core.CheckBlock
documentation:
Context independent CBlock checks.

CheckBlockHeader() is called first, which may raise a CheckBlockHeader
exception, followed the block tests. CheckTransaction() is called for every
transaction.

fCheckPoW        - Check proof-of-work.

fCheckMerkleRoot - Check merkle root and witness merkle root matches transactions.
                 - Check witness commitment in coinbase

cur_time         - Current time. Defaults to time.time()

classes.name: bitcoin.core.ValidationError
documentation:
Base class for all blockchain validation errors

Everything that is related to validating the blockchain, blocks,
transactions, scripts, etc. is derived from this class.

classes.name: bitcoin.core.COutPoint

Create an immutable copy of an existing OutPoint

returned directly.
The combination of a transaction hash and an index n into its vout

A mutable COutPoint

Create an immutable copy of an existing TxIn

If txin is already immutable (txin.__class__ is CTxIn) it is returned
directly.

An input of a transaction

Contains the location of the previous transaction's output that it claims,
and a signature that matches the output's public key.

classes.name: bitcoin.core.CMutableTxIn

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CMutableTxIn.from_txin
documentation: Create a fully mutable copy of an existing TxIn

classes.name: bitcoin.core.CTxOut

documentation:
Create an immutable copy of an existing TxOut

If txout is already immutable (txout.__class__ is CTxOut) then it will
be returned directly.

An output of a transaction

Contains the public key that the next input must be able to sign with to
claim it.

classes.name: bitcoin.core.CMutableTxOut

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CMutableTxOut.from_txout
documentation: Create a fullly mutable copy of an existing TxOut
A mutable CTxOut

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CTxWitness.from_txwitness

classes.name: bitcoin.core.CTransaction

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CTransaction.__init__
documentation:
Create a new transaction

vin and vout are iterables of transaction inputs and outputs
respectively. If their contents are not already immutable, immutable
copies will be made.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CTransaction.stream_deserialize
documentation:
Deserialize transaction

This implementation corresponds to Bitcoin's SerializeTransaction() and
consensus behavior. Note that Bitcoin's DecodeHexTx() also has the
option to attempt deserializing as a non-witness transaction first,
falling back to the consensus behavior if it fails. The difference lies
in transactions which have zero inputs: they are invalid but may be
(de)serialized anyway for the purpose of signing them and adding
inputs. If the behavior of DecodeHexTx() is needed it could be added,
but not here.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CTransaction.has_witness
documentation: True if witness

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CTransaction.from_tx
documentation:
Create an immutable copy of a pre-existing transaction

If tx is already immutable (tx.__class__ is CTransaction) then it will
be returned directly.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CTransaction.GetTxid
documentation:
Get the transaction ID.

This differs from the transactions hash as given by GetHash. GetTxid
excludes witness data, while GetHash includes it.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CTransaction.calc_weight
documentation:
Calculate the transaction weight, as defined by BIP141.

The transaction must contain at least one input and one output.
A transaction

classes.name: bitcoin.core.CBlock

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CBlock.build_merkle_tree_from_txids
documentation:
Build a full CBlock merkle tree from txids

txids - iterable of txids

Returns a new merkle tree in deepest first order. The last element is
the merkle root.

WARNING! If you're reading this because you're learning about crypto
and/or designing a new system that will use merkle trees, keep in mind
that the following merkle tree algorithm has a serious flaw related to
duplicate txids, resulting in a vulnerability. (CVE-2012-2459) Bitcoin
has since worked around the flaw, but for new applications you should
use something different; don't just copy-and-paste this code without
understanding the problem first.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CBlock.build_merkle_tree_from_txs
documentation: Build a full merkle tree from transactions

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CBlock.calc_merkle_root

Calculate the merkle root

The calculated merkle root is not cached; every invocation
re-calculates it from scratch.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.CBlock.calc_witness_merkle_root

The calculated merkle root is not cached; every invocation

functions.name: bitcoin.core.script.FindAndDelete
documentation: Consensus critical, see FindAndDelete() in Satoshi codebase

functions.name: bitcoin.core.script.IsLowDERSignature
documentation:
Loosely correlates with IsLowDERSignature() from script/interpreter.cpp
Verifies that the S value in a DER signature is the lowest possible value.
Used by BIP62 malleability fixes.

functions.name: bitcoin.core.script.CompareBigEndian
documentation:
Loosely matches CompareBigEndian() from eccryptoverify.cpp
Compares two arrays of bytes, and returns a negative value if the first is
less than the second, 0 if they're equal, and a positive value if the
first is greater than the second.

functions.name: bitcoin.core.script.RawSignatureHash
documentation:
Consensus-correct SignatureHash

Returns (hash, err) to precisely match the consensus-critical behavior of
the SIGHASH_SINGLE bug. (inIdx is *not* checked for validity)

If you're just writing wallet software you probably want SignatureHash()
instead.

functions.name: bitcoin.core.script.SignatureHash
documentation:
Calculate a signature hash

'Cooked' version that checks if inIdx is out of bounds - this is *not*
consensus-correct behavior, but is what you probably want for general
wallet use.

classes.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScriptOp

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScriptOp.encode_op_pushdata
documentation: Encode a PUSHDATA op, returning bytes

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScriptOp.encode_op_n
documentation: Encode a small integer op, returning an opcode

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScriptOp.decode_op_n
documentation: Decode a small integer opcode, returning an integer

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScriptOp.is_small_int
documentation: Return true if the op pushes a small integer to the stack
A single script opcode

classes.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScriptInvalidError
documentation: Base class for CScript exceptions

classes.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScriptTruncatedPushDataError
documentation: Invalid pushdata due to truncation

classes.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.raw_iter
documentation:
Raw iteration

Yields tuples of (opcode, data, sop_idx) so that the different possible
PUSHDATA encodings can be accurately distinguished, as well as
determining the exact opcode byte indexes. (sop_idx)

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.__iter__
documentation:
'Cooked' iteration

Returns either a CScriptOP instance, an integer, or bytes, as
appropriate.

See raw_iter() if you need to distinguish the different possible
PUSHDATA encodings.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.is_p2sh
documentation:
Test if the script is a p2sh scriptPubKey

Note that this test is consensus-critical.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.is_witness_scriptpubkey
documentation:
Returns true if this is a scriptpubkey signaling segregated witness data.

A witness program is any valid CScript that consists of a 1-byte push opcode
followed by a data push between 2 and 40 bytes.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.witness_version
documentation: Returns the witness version on [0,16].

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.is_witness_v0_keyhash
documentation: Returns true if this is a scriptpubkey for V0 P2WPKH.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.is_witness_v0_nested_keyhash
documentation: Returns true if this is a scriptSig for V0 P2WPKH embedded in P2SH.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.is_witness_v0_scripthash
documentation: Returns true if this is a scriptpubkey for V0 P2WSH.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.is_witness_v0_nested_scripthash
documentation: Returns true if this is a scriptSig for V0 P2WSH embedded in P2SH.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.is_push_only
documentation:
Test if the script only contains pushdata ops

Note that this test is consensus-critical.

Scripts that contain invalid pushdata ops return False, matching the
behavior in Bitcoin Core.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.has_canonical_pushes
documentation:
Test if script only uses canonical pushes

Not yet consensus critical; may be in the future.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.is_unspendable
documentation: Test if the script is provably unspendable

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.is_valid
documentation:
Return True if the script is valid, False otherwise

The script is valid if all PUSHDATA's are valid; invalid opcodes do not
make is_valid() return False.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.to_p2sh_scriptPubKey
documentation:
Create P2SH scriptPubKey from this redeemScript

That is, create the P2SH scriptPubKey that requires this script as a
redeemScript to spend.

checksize - Check if the redeemScript is larger than the 520-byte max
pushdata limit; raise ValueError if limit exceeded.

Since a >520-byte PUSHDATA makes EvalScript() fail, it's not actually
possible to redeem P2SH outputs with redeem scripts >520 bytes.

methods.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScript.GetSigOpCount
documentation:
Get the SigOp count.

fAccurate - Accurately count CHECKMULTISIG, see BIP16 for details.

Note that this is consensus-critical.

Serialized script

A bytes subclass, so you can use this directly whenever bytes are accepted.
Note that this means that indexing does *not* work - you'll get an index by
byte rather than opcode. This format was chosen for efficiency so that the
general case would not require creating a lot of little CScriptOP objects.

iter(script) however does iterate by opcode.

classes.name: bitcoin.core.script.CScriptWitness
documentation:
An encoding of the data elements on the initial stack for (segregated
witness)
